mission.title: Install and Configure Apache APISIX on Kubernetes
mission.description: Apache APISIX is a dynamic, real-time, high-performance API gateway. It provides traffic management features including load balancing, dynamic upstream, canary release, circuit breaking, authentication, and observability. APISIX can handle north-south traffic as well as east-west traffic between services and can also run as a Kubernetes ingress controller.

steps.title: Add the APISIX Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Apache APISIX Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add apisix https://apache.github.io/apisix-helm-chart
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install APISIX using Helm
steps.description:
Install APISIX in a new namespace called 'ingress-apisix' using the upstream chart. The chart bundles etcd as a dependency for APISIX's configuration store.
```bash
helm install apisix apisix/apisix --version 2.13.0 --namespace ingress-apisix --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the APISIX and etcd pods are running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace ingress-apisix
```

steps.title: Check the gateway service
steps.description:
Confirm the APISIX gateway Service has been created. By default it is of type LoadBalancer.
```bash
kubectl get svc apisix-gateway --namespace ingress-apisix
```

steps.title: Access the Admin API
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to reach the APISIX Admin API from your local machine. The chart ships with a default admin key that you should rotate before using in production.
```bash
kubectl port-forward -n ingress-apisix svc/apisix-admin 9180:9180
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the APISIX release from the 'ingress-apisix' namespace.
```bash
helm uninstall apisix --namespace ingress-apisix
```

uninstall.title: Clean up persistent resources
uninstall.description:
The bundled etcd runs as a StatefulSet with persistent volume claims. Delete them so the storage is released.
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace ingress-apisix
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'ingress-apisix' namespace and verify it has been removed.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace ingress-apisix
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Export the current Helm values before upgrading so you can restore any custom configuration if needed.
```bash
helm get values apisix --namespace ingress-apisix > apisix-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Refresh the APISIX Helm repository to pick up the latest chart version.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade APISIX
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the release to the next chart version. Chart 2.14.0 ships APISIX 3.16.0, a minor version bump from 3.15.0 in chart 2.13.0.
```bash
helm upgrade apisix apisix/apisix --namespace ingress-apisix --version 2.14.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the APISIX pods are running the new version.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace ingress-apisix
kubectl get pods -n ingress-apisix -l app.kubernetes.io/name=apisix -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].image}'
```

troubleshooting.title: APISIX pods stuck waiting for etcd
troubleshooting.description:
If the APISIX pods stay in Init or CrashLoopBackOff, check the etcd pods first. APISIX will not start until etcd is reachable and healthy.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n ingress-apisix -l app.kubernetes.io/name=etcd
kubectl logs -n ingress-apisix -l app.kubernetes.io/name=etcd --tail=100
```

troubleshooting.title: Admin API returning 401
troubleshooting.description:
APISIX requires the X-API-KEY header for Admin API requests. The default key is defined in the chart values under 'apisix.admin.credentials.admin'. Confirm the key you are using matches the one in the cluster.
```bash
kubectl get -n ingress-apisix secret apisix -o jsonpath='{.data}'
helm get values apisix --namespace ingress-apisix
```

troubleshooting.title: Gateway service stuck in Pending
troubleshooting.description:
If the apisix-gateway Service stays Pending, your cluster may not have a LoadBalancer provider. Either install one (MetalLB on bare metal) or override the service type.
```bash
kubectl describe svc apisix-gateway -n ingress-apisix
helm upgrade apisix apisix/apisix --namespace ingress-apisix --set service.type=NodePort
```

troubleshooting.title: Routes not taking effect
troubleshooting.description:
If routes configured through the Admin API are not being served, check the APISIX worker logs for syntax or plugin errors.
```bash
kubectl logs -n ingress-apisix -l app.kubernetes.io/name=apisix --tail=200
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of APISIX will show both the APISIX and etcd pods running in the 'ingress-apisix' namespace, an apisix-gateway Service reachable on its external IP or NodePort, and an Admin API accepting requests with the configured X-API-KEY. You can then register upstream services and routes through the Admin API or the ingress controller.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add apisix https://apache.github.io/apisix-helm-chart
helm repo update

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine. The chart bundles etcd by default, so a StorageClass must be available in the cluster for the etcd PersistentVolumeClaims.
